topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

I scrolled through my feed, a familiar, tight knot forming in my chest. Another notification from 'StarlightSeeker,' my online confidante, who always seemed to exist in some distant, sun-drenched locale. I’d been meticulously avoiding the urge to reply to their latest cryptic message for three days. It felt like a deliberate strategy, a way to maintain a certain distance when my reality felt too cluttered. Then, I saw it: a geotagged photo of a coffee shop downtown. My stomach clenched. StarlightSeeker had been posting from *my* city. The realization hit me with a jarring force, making my fingers freeze over the screen. I stared at the photo, then back at the profile, a strange mix of disbelief and sudden, unwelcome recognition washing over me.

[story 2]

The silence in my apartment was usually a comfort, a blank canvas after a day spent navigating the complexities of my real life. I was determined to keep things exactly as they were—separate, contained. But then the DM arrived. It was StarlightSeeker, an unexpected ping in the quiet. Their message was simple, a casual mention of a local event I’d been meaning to check out. A slow burn of internal resistance started, a stubborn refusal to acknowledge the possibility that this digital tether might have a physical anchor. I leaned back in my chair, rubbing my temples. Why would they be here? The thought felt irritatingly intrusive, demanding an explanation that I wasn't prepared to give.

[story 3]

It started subtly, a shared reference to a local landmark that only someone who truly *lived* here would know. I dismissed it at first, chalking it up to coincidence, a fluke of algorithmic suggestion. But then StarlightSeeker tagged a picture of the exact park bench where I’d spent an entire rainy afternoon last Tuesday. A deep, slow simmer began beneath my skin. I couldn't shake the feeling of being deliberately kept at arm's length, a barrier I was actively constructing. I typed a reply, carefully choosing my words to sound breezy, masking the sudden, sharp awareness that the person I thought was just a voice on a screen was, in fact, breathing the same air as me.
